---
title: "When the Economy Turns Grey, So Does Fashion"
subtitle: "A Quantitative Analysis of Fashion Color Trends and Economic Conditions"
author: "Maria Workman"
format:
  pdf:
    documentclass: article
    classoption: [11pt]
    geometry:
      - margin=1in
    colorlinks: true
    toc: false
    number-sections: false
    fig-pos: "H"
jupyter: python3
execute:
  echo: false
  warning: false
  message: false
header-includes:
  - \usepackage{titlesec}
  - \usepackage{parskip}
  - \usepackage{float}
  - \usepackage{setspace}
  - \usepackage{booktabs}
  - \usepackage{longtable}
  - \usepackage{array}
  - \usepackage{fancyhdr}
  - \usepackage{xcolor}
  - \usepackage{graphicx}
  - \usepackage{caption}
  - \usepackage{amsmath}
  - \usepackage{helvet}
  - \renewcommand{\familydefault}{\sfdefault}
  - \setstretch{1.15}
  - \titleformat{\section}{\Large\bfseries}{}{0em}{}
  - \titleformat{\subsection}{\normalsize\bfseries}{}{0em}{}
  - \captionsetup{font=small,labelfont=bf}
  - \pagestyle{fancy}
  - \fancyhf{}
  - \fancyfoot[C]{\thepage}
  - \setlength{\headheight}{14pt}
---

***Done***

1. Executive Summmary 

2. Introduction 

3. Constructing a Measure of "Neutrality"

4. Validating the Metric 

***To Do***

5. Data and Economic Variables 

6. Empirical Strategy 

7. Results 

8. Lagged Effects 

9. Discussion. 

10. Conclusion 



1. Executive Summary

Fashion consumer data has long served as a barometer of economic sentiment, with trends such as rising lipstick sales or longer hemlines often interpreted as signals of recession.

These patterns—sometimes described as the “lipstick effect,” in which consumers shift toward smaller, more affordable luxuries during downturns—have been widely discussed in both industry and financial contexts.^[For a general overview of the lipstick effect, see: https://www.chase.com/personal/investments/learning-and-insights/article/what-is-the-lipstick-index]

Yet such observations have largely remained anecdotal. Increasingly, however, both consumers and fashion commentators have begun to notice a broader aesthetic shift: in periods of uncertainty, fashion appears to move toward muted, neutral palettes and understated design.

This intuition is echoed in contemporary fashion discourse, where seasonal palettes and runway trends are frequently described as becoming more subdued in tone during uncertain periods.^[See, for example: https://www.fashiontimes.com/runway-color-trends-everyones-talking-about-according-fashion-week-colors-seasonal-palettes-13552]

This project takes a more systematic approach. Using machine learning and computer vision, I analyze fashion imagery at scale to quantify shifts in color trends over time.

The central question is simple: when the economic outlook becomes less bright, does fashion follow suit? Specifically, is there a measurable relationship between the prevalence of neutral color palettes and recession indicators?

2. Introduction 

# Research Question

Does fashion become more neutral when economic conditions worsen?

# Hypothesis

The central hypothesis of this analysis is that worsening economic conditions are associated with lower average chroma in fashion imagery. In practical terms, this implies that periods of higher unemployment or recession correspond with more visually muted or neutral color palettes.

# Data and Measures

The analysis combines two types of data:

- Fashion color data, measured using average chroma extracted from fashion images  
- Macroeconomic data, including annual unemployment rates and recession indicators  

The dependent variable is:

- **Mean chroma**: a proxy for how vivid or saturated the average fashion color palette is in a given year, constructed using unsupervised machine learning on up to 100 images per year sampled from the Internet Archive’s *Vogue Runway* dataset  

Machine learning enables the processing and analysis of large-scale visual datasets that would be difficult to quantify manually.^[For an example of large-scale fashion image analysis using machine learning, see: https://arxiv.org/abs/1608.07444]

The independent variables include:

- Unemployment rate  
- Lagged economic indicators, where relevant  
- Year, included in some models as a control for long-run time trends  

# Methodology

To evaluate the relationship between economic conditions and fashion color trends, I estimate a set of linear regression models:

- A model using unemployment and recession simultaneously  
- A model controlling for year  
- A lagged model using prior-year economic conditions  

These models are not intended to establish definitive causal relationships, but rather to provide an exploratory framework for assessing whether a measurable association exists between macroeconomic stress and aesthetic change.

3. Constructing a Measure of Neutrality 

# Constructing a Measure of Neutrality

Before extracting color information, I applied background removal to better isolate the clothing itself from distracting elements such as runway backdrops, lighting, and audience noise.

I used Python’s `rembg` library, which leverages deep learning–based segmentation to automatically remove image backgrounds and retain the foreground subject. This step is important because background colors can significantly skew palette extraction, especially in runway photography where staging and lighting vary widely.

Each image was then processed to extract its dominant color palette using *k*-means clustering, an unsupervised machine learning technique that groups similar pixels based on color similarity. I set \( k = 8 \), meaning that each image is represented by eight dominant color clusters, which together approximate its overall palette.

From these extracted palettes, I construct two complementary metrics to quantify how visually “neutral” a given look is.

- **Mean chroma**, which captures the average color intensity across the palette. Lower chroma values correspond to more muted, desaturated tones—such as greys, blacks, and earth tones—while higher values indicate more vivid, saturated colors.  

- **Neutral share**, defined as the proportion of palette colors that fall below a specified chroma threshold. This metric captures the extent to which a look is dominated by neutral tones, rather than simply averaging intensity. While mean chroma reflects overall vibrancy, neutral share isolates the prevalence of subdued colors within the composition.  

Full implementation details, including image preprocessing and clustering procedures, are available in the accompanying repository.

I then aggregate the data by year. The results are shown below.

```{python}
# load results 

import pandas as pd
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

metrics_df = pd.read_csv("/Users/mariaworkman/fashion/fashion-neutrality/data/results/metrics_full_run.csv")

# keep only valid rows
metrics_df = metrics_df[metrics_df["ok"] == True]

# aggregate by year 

yearly = (
    metrics_df
    .groupby("year")
    .agg(
        mean_chroma=("mean_chroma", "mean"),
        neutral_share=("neutral_share", "mean"),
        count=("mean_chroma", "size")
    )
    .reset_index()
    .sort_values("year")
)

# plot mean chroma over years 

plt.figure()
plt.plot(yearly["year"], yearly["mean_chroma"])
plt.xlabel("Year")
plt.ylabel("Mean Chroma")
plt.title("Mean Chroma Over Time")
plt.show()

# plot neutral share over years (could be pretty messy, need to adjust cutoff for neutrality)

plt.figure()
plt.plot(yearly["year"], yearly["neutral_share"])
plt.xlabel("Year")
plt.ylabel("Neutral Color Share")
plt.title("Neutral Share Over Time")
plt.show()

```

As expected, mean chroma and neutral share

```{python}

# distribution checks 

plt.figure()
plt.hist(metrics_df["mean_chroma"], bins=40)
plt.xlabel("Mean Chroma")
plt.ylabel("Count")
plt.title("Distribution of Mean Chroma")
plt.show()

plt.figure()
plt.hist(metrics_df["neutral_share"], bins=40)
plt.xlabel("Neutral Share")
plt.ylabel("Count")
plt.title("Distribution of Neutral Share")
plt.show()
```


Are these changes statistically significant, or simply noise? I performed a t-test to find out.

```{python}
from scipy.stats import ttest_ind

# define periods
low_period = metrics_df[metrics_df["year"].between(2006, 2009)]["mean_chroma"]
high_period = metrics_df[metrics_df["year"].between(2017, 2020)]["mean_chroma"]

# check sizes (important)
print("Low n:", len(low_period))
print("High n:", len(high_period))

# Welch’s t-test
t_stat, p_value = ttest_ind(high_period, low_period, equal_var=False)

print("\nLow period mean:", low_period.mean())
print("High period mean:", high_period.mean())
print("T-statistic:", t_stat)
print("P-value:", p_value)
```

Having established that chroma meaningfully captures variation in fashion aesthetics, I next examine whether these variations are associated with macroeconomic conditions.

4. Validating the Metric

# Validating the Metric

*[Commentary: Interpret the t-test results above. The test compares mean chroma in 2006–2009 (a low chroma period) versus 2017–2020 (a higher chroma period). Discuss what a statistically significant result implies about chroma as a meaningful signal of aesthetic variation over time.]*

5. Data and Economic Variables

# Data and Economic Variables

*[Commentary: Describe the data sources — Vogue Runway imagery from the Internet Archive for the fashion side, and FRED (Federal Reserve Economic Data) for unemployment and recession indicators. Note the time range and any coverage gaps.]*

```{python}
import pandas as pd
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
from pathlib import Path

unemp_path = Path("/Users/mariaworkman/fashion/fashion-neutrality/data/econ/UNRATE.csv")
recession_path = Path("/Users/mariaworkman/fashion/fashion-neutrality/data/econ/USREC.csv")

# download once and cache locally; load from disk on subsequent renders
if not unemp_path.exists():
    unemp_path.parent.mkdir(parents=True, exist_ok=True)
    import requests
    unemp_path.write_text(requests.get(
        "https://fred.stlouisfed.org/graph/fredgraph.csv?id=UNRATE",
        headers={"User-Agent": "research-script/1.0"}
    ).text)

if not recession_path.exists():
    import requests
    recession_path.write_text(requests.get(
        "https://fred.stlouisfed.org/graph/fredgraph.csv?id=USREC",
        headers={"User-Agent": "research-script/1.0"}
    ).text)

unemp = pd.read_csv(unemp_path)
unemp["year"] = pd.to_datetime(unemp["DATE"]).dt.year
yearly_unemp = (
    unemp
    .groupby("year")
    .agg(unemployment_rate=("UNRATE", "mean"))
    .reset_index()
)

recession = pd.read_csv(recession_path)
recession["year"] = pd.to_datetime(recession["DATE"]).dt.year
yearly_recession = (
    recession
    .groupby("year")
    .agg(recession_share=("USREC", "mean"))
    .reset_index()
)

# merge with fashion data and restrict to study period
merged = (
    yearly
    .merge(yearly_unemp, on="year", how="left")
    .merge(yearly_recession, on="year", how="left")
    .sort_values("year")
)
merged = merged[(merged["year"] >= 2000) & (merged["year"] <= 2023)].copy()

# construct lag and change variables on full series before any dropna
merged["unemp_lag1"] = merged["unemployment_rate"].shift(1)
merged["recession_lag1"] = merged["recession_share"].shift(1)
merged["unemp_change"] = merged["unemployment_rate"].diff()
merged["chroma_change"] = merged["mean_chroma"].diff()
merged["unemp_change_lag1"] = merged["unemp_change"].shift(1)

print(merged[["year", "mean_chroma", "unemployment_rate", "recession_share"]].head(10))
```

6. Empirical Strategy

# Empirical Strategy

*[Commentary: Describe the regression approach — OLS with mean chroma as the dependent variable. Explain why unemployment rate and recession share are the key predictors. Note that year is included in some models as a control for long-run stylistic trends unrelated to the economy. Clarify that the goal is exploratory association, not causal identification.]*

7. Results

# Results

```{python}
from sklearn.linear_model import LinearRegression
from sklearn.metrics import r2_score

results = []
df = merged.copy()
```

## Model 1: Unemployment and Recession

```{python}
model_df = df.dropna(subset=["mean_chroma", "unemployment_rate", "recession_share"]).copy()

X1 = model_df[["unemployment_rate", "recession_share"]]
y1 = model_df["mean_chroma"]

model1 = LinearRegression()
model1.fit(X1, y1)

results.append({
    "Model": "Unemployment + Recession",
    "Intercept": round(model1.intercept_, 4),
    "Unemployment Coef": round(model1.coef_[0], 4),
    "Recession Coef": round(model1.coef_[1], 4),
    "R²": round(r2_score(y1, model1.predict(X1)), 4)
})

pd.DataFrame(results)
```

*[Commentary: Interpret the coefficients. A negative unemployment coefficient would suggest that higher unemployment is associated with lower mean chroma — i.e., more neutral fashion. Discuss magnitude and direction of both predictors.]*

## Model 2: Controlling for Year

```{python}
model_df2 = df.dropna(subset=["mean_chroma", "unemployment_rate", "recession_share", "year"]).copy()

X2 = model_df2[["unemployment_rate", "recession_share", "year"]]
y2 = model_df2["mean_chroma"]

model2 = LinearRegression()
model2.fit(X2, y2)

results.append({
    "Model": "Unemployment + Recession + Year",
    "Intercept": round(model2.intercept_, 4),
    "Unemployment Coef": round(model2.coef_[0], 4),
    "Recession Coef": round(model2.coef_[1], 4),
    "Year Coef": round(model2.coef_[2], 4),
    "R²": round(r2_score(y2, model2.predict(X2)), 4)
})

pd.DataFrame(results)
```

*[Commentary: Discuss how adding year as a control changes the economic coefficients. If the effects remain negative, that strengthens the interpretation — the relationship is not simply a byproduct of a general long-run trend in fashion color.]*

## Visualizing the Relationship

```{python}
# actual vs. predicted (model with year)
plt.figure(figsize=(10, 5))
plt.plot(model_df2["year"], y2, marker="o", label="Actual mean chroma")
plt.plot(model_df2["year"], model2.predict(X2), marker="o", linestyle="--", label="Predicted (with year)")
plt.xlabel("Year")
plt.ylabel("Mean Chroma")
plt.title("Actual vs. Predicted Mean Chroma")
plt.legend()
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()
```

```{python}
# chroma and economic indicators over time (dual axis)
fig, ax1 = plt.subplots(figsize=(10, 5))

ax1.plot(df["year"], df["mean_chroma"], marker="o", label="Mean Chroma")
ax1.set_xlabel("Year")
ax1.set_ylabel("Mean Chroma")

ax2 = ax1.twinx()
ax2.plot(df["year"], df["unemployment_rate"], marker="o", linestyle="--", label="Unemployment Rate")
ax2.plot(df["year"], df["recession_share"], marker="o", linestyle=":", label="Recession Share")
ax2.set_ylabel("Economic Indicators")

lines1, labels1 = ax1.get_legend_handles_labels()
lines2, labels2 = ax2.get_legend_handles_labels()
ax1.legend(lines1 + lines2, labels1 + labels2, loc="best")

plt.title("Mean Chroma vs. Economic Indicators")
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()
```

```{python}
# scatter: chroma vs. unemployment rate
plt.figure(figsize=(8, 4.5))
plt.scatter(df["unemployment_rate"], df["mean_chroma"])
plt.xlabel("Unemployment Rate")
plt.ylabel("Mean Chroma")
plt.title("Mean Chroma vs. Unemployment Rate")
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()
```

*[Commentary: Discuss what the visualizations reveal. Do peaks in unemployment visually align with dips in chroma? Note any outlier years or periods where the pattern breaks down.]*

8. Lagged Effects

# Lagged Effects

*[Commentary: Motivate the lagged analysis — design cycles, production lead times, and trend diffusion suggest that fashion may respond to economic conditions with a delay rather than immediately. Discuss what a significant lagged effect would imply about the mechanism.]*

## Model 3: Lagged Recession

```{python}
lag_df = df.dropna(subset=["mean_chroma", "unemployment_rate", "recession_lag1", "year"]).copy()

X3 = lag_df[["unemployment_rate", "recession_lag1", "year"]]
y3 = lag_df["mean_chroma"]

model3 = LinearRegression()
model3.fit(X3, y3)

results.append({
    "Model": "Unemployment + Lagged Recession + Year",
    "Intercept": round(model3.intercept_, 4),
    "Unemployment Coef": round(model3.coef_[0], 4),
    "Lagged Recession Coef": round(model3.coef_[1], 4),
    "Year Coef": round(model3.coef_[2], 4),
    "R²": round(r2_score(y3, model3.predict(X3)), 4)
})

pd.DataFrame(results)
```

```{python}
plt.figure(figsize=(10, 5))
plt.plot(lag_df["year"], y3, marker="o", label="Actual mean chroma")
plt.plot(lag_df["year"], model3.predict(X3), marker="o", linestyle="--", label="Predicted (lagged recession)")
plt.xlabel("Year")
plt.ylabel("Mean Chroma")
plt.title("Actual vs. Predicted Mean Chroma (Lagged Recession Model)")
plt.legend()
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()
```

## Model 4: Lagged Unemployment Change

```{python}
lag_change_df = df.dropna(subset=["chroma_change", "unemp_change_lag1"]).copy()

X_lag_c = lag_change_df[["unemp_change_lag1"]]
y_lag_c = lag_change_df["chroma_change"]

model_lag_c = LinearRegression()
model_lag_c.fit(X_lag_c, y_lag_c)

print("--- LAG CHANGE MODEL: ΔChroma ~ ΔUnemployment (t-1) ---")
print("Intercept:", round(model_lag_c.intercept_, 4))
print("unemp_change_lag1:", round(model_lag_c.coef_[0], 4))
print("R²:", round(model_lag_c.score(X_lag_c, y_lag_c), 4))
```

*[Commentary: Discuss the lagged change model. This tests whether a year-over-year increase in unemployment predicts a decrease in chroma the following year. Compare with the level models above — does the lagged change specification tell a different story?]*

9. Discussion

# Discussion

*[Commentary: Synthesize results across all models. Address: (1) whether the evidence supports the hypothesis, (2) what the coefficient magnitudes imply in practical terms, (3) which model specification is most convincing and why, (4) alternative explanations for the observed patterns, and (5) what additional data or methods would strengthen these findings.]*

10. Conclusion

# Conclusion

*[Commentary: Summarize the key findings and their implications. Restate the central question and what the analysis revealed. Note what the results suggest about the relationship between economic conditions and aesthetic change in fashion — and what remains open for future work.]*

# Appendix: Regression Summary Table

```{python}
pd.DataFrame(results)
```

# Appendix: Correlation Matrix

```{python}
numeric_cols = [col for col in ["mean_chroma", "unemployment_rate", "recession_share", "unemp_lag1", "recession_lag1", "year"] if col in df.columns]
df[numeric_cols].corr(numeric_only=True)
```
